suggestions.name: StringComparator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive comparison unit that implements various string comparison strategies with null-safe handling. Extracting them into a StringComparator class creates a focused component that encapsulates all string comparison logic, improving code organization and enabling reuse in sorting and ordering operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: comparing two strings and returning an integer result following the standard Java comparison contract (-1, 0, 1). They handle null-safe string comparison with options for case sensitivity and null ordering behavior.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string comparison" domain. They implement the Comparator pattern for strings, providing various comparison strategies (case-sensitive, case-insensitive, with configurable null handling). This is a cohesive set of comparison operations that work together to provide comprehensive string ordering functionality.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string comparison logic from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Can be used as a standalone comparator or injected into sorting algorithms
- Reduces Complexity: Removes comparison concerns from the main StringUtils class
- Improves Testability: Comparison logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate comparison concerns. This creates a specialized component that implements comparison strategies, following the Strategy pattern. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods perform string comparison) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, only requires String operations).

suggestions.name: CaseConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on string case transformation operations. Extracting them into a CaseConverter class improves separation of concerns by isolating case conversion logic from general string utilities, while providing both basic and locale-aware conversion capabilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: transforming the case of strings. They collectively provide case conversion functionality, specifically converting strings to lowercase and uppercase with optional locale support. All methods handle null input gracefully and delegate to Java's built-in string case conversion methods.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "case conversion" domain within string manipulation. They represent a cohesive subsystem focused solely on changing the capitalization of text, with locale-aware variants for internationalization support. This is a well-defined transformation operation on strings.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Case conversion logic is isolated from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Case converter can be used independently by classes that only need case transformation
- Reduces Complexity: Removes case conversion concerns from the larger StringUtils class
- Better Testability: Case conversion logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate case conversion concerns. This creates a specialized converter class with high cohesion (all methods perform case transformations) and low coupling (minimal dependencies, only requires LocaleUtils). The extraction follows the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated to one specific type of string transformation.

suggestions.name: StringAugmenter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on augmenting strings by conditionally adding missing prefixes or suffixes. They share the common responsibility of ensuring strings contain required character sequences without duplication, making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized augmentation class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: conditionally adding prefixes or suffixes to strings only when they are missing. They perform string augmentation operations that ensure specific character sequences appear at the beginning or end of strings without duplication.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string augmentation" domain. They represent operations that extend strings by adding missing parts, with both case-sensitive and case-insensitive variants. This is a specialized subset of string manipulation focused specifically on ensuring strings have required prefixes or suffixes.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string augmentation logic from general string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: String augmentation operations can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: Removes specialized augmentation logic from the large StringUtils class
- Better Testability: Focused class allows for more targeted testing of augmentation scenarios

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related operations. These methods form a natural grouping around the concept of "ensuring strings have required prefixes/suffixes" which is distinct from general string manipulation utilities.
